title: How to Speak in an MLE Interview

sections.heading: Structure First
sections.body: Open with the shape of your answer: problem, constraints, approach, trade-offs, and validation. A concise map makes your reasoning easier to follow.

sections.heading: Think Out Loud
sections.body: Narrate assumptions and checks as you go. Interviewers are often evaluating how you debug ambiguity, not only whether you remember a specific API.

sections.heading: Use STAR Without Sounding Scripted
sections.body: For experience questions, frame the situation, task, action, and result. Keep the result measurable when possible: latency cut, F1 lift, cost reduction, fewer incidents, or faster iteration.

sections.heading: Name Trade-offs
bullets: Accuracy versus latency
Recall versus precision
Simple validation versus deployment-realistic validation
Model complexity versus maintainability
Offline metric gains versus production monitoring risk

sections.heading: Tell Optimization Stories
sections.body: Strong MLE stories often involve finding a bottleneck, proving the root cause, and making the system better without overcomplicating it.

sections.heading: Frame Projects
bullets: Problem: what user or business outcome mattered?
Approach: what model, data, and validation choices did you make?
Debugging: what broke, leaked, overfit, or slowed down?
Impact: what changed in metrics, reliability, cost, or iteration speed?

sections.heading: Good Signals
sections.body: Mention metric choice, leakage prevention, class imbalance, model serving constraints, monitoring, retraining triggers, and how you validated that an improvement was real.

sections.heading: Model Evaluation
bullets: Explain why accuracy can fail under imbalance.
Choose macro, micro, or weighted metrics based on class importance.
Discuss threshold tuning as a product or risk decision.

sections.heading: Validation Pitfalls
bullets: Fit preprocessing only on training data.
Use time-aware splits for forecasting or event streams.
Use group-aware splits when users, accounts, patients, or devices repeat.

sections.heading: Debugging Training
bullets: Check loss API expectations, target shape, and dtype.
Verify optimizer.zero_grad, learning rate, gradient norms, and scheduler timing.
Separate train and eval mode behavior.

sections.heading: Performance Optimization
sections.body: Look for row-wise Python loops, repeated device transfers, non-batched inference, wasteful serialization, and unnecessary recomputation.

sections.heading: Do
bullets: Clarify the objective before optimizing.
State assumptions and expected failure modes.
Tie validation to production reality.
Use metrics that reflect the cost of errors.
Describe how you would monitor the model after launch.

sections.heading: Don't
bullets: Optimize an offline metric without checking leakage.
Treat a 0.5 threshold as automatically correct.
Ignore class-level performance under imbalance.
Forget train/eval mode, no-grad inference, or gradient clearing.
Jump to a larger model before checking data and training bugs.
